title: Sleeping With The Enemy

jokes.text: 68 squares of ankle-busting fury. That's right, A double-dogleg, hamstring knotting Hop-Way to Hell.
jokes.setup: Character describing a hopscotch course
jokes.punchline: Over-the-top dramatic language for a children's game
jokes.explanation: Clever juxtaposition of extreme action movie language with innocent playground equipment. Well-crafted wordplay with 'Hop-Way to Hell' being particularly inventive.

jokes.text: What insane kangaroo dreamed this fevered nightmare?
jokes.setup: Continuing the dramatic description of hopscotch
jokes.punchline: Attributing the invention of hopscotch to an 'insane kangaroo'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic connecting kangaroos to hopscotch through the hopping motion. Escalates the dramatic language perfectly.

jokes.text: What? - Nothing.
jokes.setup: Someone says something awkward about getting pebbles
jokes.punchline: The uncomfortable non-response
jokes.explanation: Standard awkward silence beat. Functional but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Lisa has a big butt.
jokes.setup: Kids being kids on the playground
jokes.punchline: Direct, blunt insult that sets up the episode's main plot
jokes.explanation: Simple setup for the episode's main conflict. Character-appropriate for Bart but not particularly creative.

jokes.text: Don't make Lisa mad. She might crush us all with her giant butt.
jokes.setup: Building on the butt insult
jokes.punchline: Exaggerating Lisa's butt to apocalyptic proportions
jokes.explanation: Good escalation technique, taking the insult to absurd extremes. Solid construction but predictable premise.

jokes.text: Stop teasing the big-bottomed lass. She's just as God made her... Plump as a Christmas goose.
jokes.setup: Willie appearing to defend Lisa
jokes.punchline: His 'defense' is actually another insult comparing her to poultry
jokes.explanation: Perfect Willie moment - his Scottish cadence, inappropriate comparison, and well-meaning but horrible delivery. Classic Simpsons character comedy where the 'help' makes things worse.

jokes.text: Every Simpson starts with a circle. Daddy has one big circle here... then a smaller one up here... two big, sexy circles for eyes...
jokes.setup: Homer trying to explain family genetics through drawing
jokes.punchline: Describing himself as 'sexy circles' while drawing crude stick figures
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer vanity combined with his terrible drawing skills. The 'sexy circles' descriptor is perfectly in character and absurd.

jokes.text: And for some reason, my hair and ear form an 'M' and a 'G.'
jokes.setup: Homer continuing his artistic self-analysis
jokes.punchline: Accidentally referencing Matt Groening's initials hidden in his design
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke that works on multiple levels - casual viewers get Homer's obliviousness, fans get the Matt Groening easter egg reference.

jokes.text: Party! Party! Party! Party!
jokes.setup: Homer getting excited about his artistic creation
jokes.punchline: His childlike celebration over stick figure drawing
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer childishness. Character-appropriate but not particularly inventive.

jokes.text: Oh, Homer, you're wasting away to nothing. You simply must eat something.
jokes.setup: Marge commenting on Homer's appearance after he ties a sweater around his waist
jokes.punchline: Describing obviously overweight Homer as 'wasting away'
jokes.explanation: Good ironic observation playing on Marge's enabling of Homer's eating habits. Classic marital dynamic humor.

jokes.text: Here's a mistake. It says the capital of Kentucky... is Frankfort. Dream on, pal.
jokes.setup: Homer checking Bart's test for authenticity
jokes.punchline: Homer is wrong about a basic fact while trying to catch Bart cheating
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - his confidence in being wrong is character-defining. Good irony structure with him trying to catch fraud while being fraudulent himself.

jokes.text: Really? Frankfurter... is that anything?
jokes.setup: Homer learning Frankfort is actually correct
jokes.punchline: His brain making a food association with 'frankfurter'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - everything eventually leads back to food. The innocent delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Bart really did get a hundred. I gave a test on state capitals, but I forgot to roll up the map. Everyone got a hundred.
jokes.setup: The mystery of how Bart got a perfect score
jokes.punchline: The teacher's incompetence enabled all students to cheat
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff to the test mystery. Subverts expectation that Bart cheated with teacher incompetence. Good economy in the reveal.

jokes.text: I win. P-A-R-T. Par-tee.
jokes.setup: Bart celebrating his victory
jokes.punchline: Spelling out 'party' like it's a cheer but missing the Y
jokes.explanation: Cute wordplay that shows Bart's enthusiasm while subtly undermining his spelling skills. Character-appropriate humor.

jokes.text: This means nothing. Yes, yes, you've made that quite clear.
jokes.setup: Apparent romantic tension between the characters
jokes.punchline: Skinner's weary response to repeated rejection
jokes.explanation: Good relationship comedy with Skinner's resigned delivery

jokes.text: I heard she's back to her birth weight. Must be nice.
jokes.setup: Background conversation about someone's weight
jokes.punchline: Envying someone for being extremely underweight
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about weight obsession culture. The deadpan delivery and absurd comparison make it effective social commentary.

jokes.text: I make four bucks an hour planing down the thighs.
jokes.setup: Worker explaining their job duties
jokes.punchline: Casually describing body modification of mannequins as routine labor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of beauty standards. The matter-of-fact delivery of grotesque work makes it darkly hilarious. Great visual comedy implied.

jokes.text: Now you've got no choice. Non!
jokes.setup: Lisa looking at herself in mirror
jokes.punchline: Someone (presumably French) rejecting her appearance immediately
jokes.explanation: Quick visual gag but relies on stereotypical French rejection. Functional but not particularly creative.

jokes.text: He sure is... if he can keep up with these party animals. Aunt Patty, Aunt Selma, Grandma Bouvier and... Grampa.
jokes.setup: Marge promising Bart will party
jokes.punchline: Revealing the 'party animals' are elderly relatives
jokes.explanation: Good ironic setup with Marge's misunderstanding of what kids want

jokes.text: Plus your school-yard chums: Martin and Ralph. Bart's my bestest boyfriend.
jokes.setup: Marge listing party guests
jokes.punchline: Ralph's immediate inappropriate declaration
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph Wiggum moment - innocent but inappropriate timing. His character-specific naivety creates the humor.

jokes.text: Rock on. You know, the first time I met Bart was at recess in first grade... Oh, I told him to use a land line.
jokes.setup: Milhouse calling in from quarantine
jokes.punchline: Phone cutting out during his heartfelt story
jokes.explanation: Classic tech failure gag. The setup with heartfelt story being interrupted works but is fairly predictable.

jokes.text: Okay, gang, let's get this party started. or should I say Barted?
jokes.setup: Marge trying to be hip for the party
jokes.punchline: Forced pun on Bart's name
jokes.explanation: Classic mom trying to be cool cringe. The bad pun is the point - Marge's awkwardness is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: I drew him with Canadian Prime Minister Louis St. Laurent. Their relationship was frosty at best.
jokes.setup: Kids asked to draw President Eisenhower
jokes.punchline: Martin's overly academic and historically detailed approach
jokes.explanation: Perfect Martin Prince moment - turning a simple kids' activity into academic overanalysis. The specific historical reference and 'frosty' pun show great character writing.

jokes.text: Duck, duck, duck, duck, duck, duck, duck... Say 'goose,' you stupid freak.
jokes.setup: Kids playing duck duck goose
jokes.punchline: Frustration building as someone won't say goose
jokes.explanation: Captures the frustration of children's games when someone doesn't play correctly. Relatable but not particularly inventive.

jokes.text: What? I'm not fat enough already? How could you say that to me?
jokes.setup: Marge offering Lisa cake
jokes.punchline: Lisa's oversensitive reaction due to her body image issues
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's sensitivity about her appearance

jokes.text: I had to leave 'cause my French bread pizza dinged.
jokes.setup: Milhouse explaining why he left the phone call
jokes.punchline: The mundane reason interrupting the 'party'
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist detail showing how pathetic the party situation is

jokes.text: Remember that maple leaf ironed between the sheets of wax paper? What was that? That was crap.
jokes.setup: Homer complaining about Bart's gifts to them
jokes.punchline: Blunt dismissal of child's craft project
jokes.explanation: Homer's brutal honesty about a child's heartfelt gift is perfectly in character. The specificity of the craft project makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Soon they'll be leaving the nest, and you'll have time for yourself. hundreds of thousands of hours, And then, the peace of the grave.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to comfort Marge about kids growing up
jokes.punchline: Escalating from free time to death
jokes.explanation: Great dark escalation from comfort to existential dread. Homer's attempt to help making things worse is classic character work.

jokes.text: Mom, after that lame party you threw, I'd rather hang out with thunder-thighs here.
jokes.setup: Marge trying to spend time with Bart
jokes.punchline: Bart preferring Lisa despite just insulting her
jokes.explanation: Continues the butt theme while showing Bart's priorities. The insult within the preference is character-appropriate cruelty.

jokes.text: Seymour! Do you want your vitamin in applesauce? Or are you gonna take it like a big boy? Applesauce.
jokes.setup: Discussion about children asserting independence
jokes.punchline: Cut to adult Skinner being babied by his mother
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic juxtaposition - cutting from independence discussion to ultimate dependence. Skinner's choice of applesauce is the perfect pathetic cherry on top.

jokes.text: Stop struggling and get in my mouth, jerks!
jokes.setup: Maggie trying to eat something
jokes.punchline: Baby's internal monologue being unexpectedly aggressive
jokes.explanation: Classic Simpsons baby humor - giving Maggie adult anger and language. The contrast between baby actions and tough talk works well.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah. Bart Simpson. Spiky hair, soft kidneys, always hitting himself?
jokes.setup: Nelson trying to identify which kid he bullies
jokes.punchline: Oddly specific and increasingly bizarre descriptors
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from normal (spiky hair) to bizarre (soft kidneys) to absurd (hitting himself). Nelson's casual delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Those tadpoles are your lunch? Technically, brunch.
jokes.setup: Marge noticing Nelson's unusual meal
jokes.punchline: Nelson's matter-of-fact correction about meal timing
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery - Nelson treating dining etiquette as more important than eating actual food. Great character establishment.

jokes.text: I have a sister... I think she's dead now who had the same problem.
jokes.setup: Nelson relating to Lisa's body image issues
jokes.punchline: Casual uncertainty about sibling's death
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - the casual 'I think she's dead now' delivered as an aside while trying to be helpful. Perfect Nelson character work combining toughness with vulnerability.

jokes.text: You're nocturnal. You don't have to impress me by making fun of others. I already like you.
jokes.setup: Marge trying to connect with Nelson
jokes.punchline: Her misuse of 'nocturnal' to mean something else entirely
jokes.explanation: Great Marge malapropism showing her good intentions but confusion

jokes.text: Whatever. Take it sleazy.
jokes.setup: Nelson ending a conversation
jokes.punchline: His version of 'take it easy'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Nelson catchphrase - tough guy corruption of polite farewell. The wordplay is simple but character-perfect.

jokes.text: Nelson? I thought you were sleeping in the park tonight. Oh, that's okay, the kid don't bother me none.
jokes.setup: Discovering Nelson at the Simpsons house
jokes.punchline: Moe's casual acceptance of a child being homeless
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor about child neglect delivered casually

jokes.text: Hey, kids, I just heard they canceled league night down at the bowling alley. You know what that means? Open lanes!
jokes.setup: Marge trying to excite kids about an activity
jokes.punchline: Bowling being her idea of exciting
jokes.explanation: Classic Marge attempt to be exciting that falls flat. Her enthusiasm for mundane activities is character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Mom, when I want lame and needy, I'll call Milhouse. No offense.
jokes.setup: Bart rejecting Marge's offer
jokes.punchline: Casual insult to friend as comparison point
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Bart's casual cruelty to friends. The 'no offense' makes it funnier by highlighting his insensitivity.

jokes.text: You've never had one before? No, but I saw some in a catalog once. People in sweaters were eating them at a picnic.
jokes.setup: Nelson's reaction to hot dogs
jokes.punchline: His only reference being an idealized catalog image
jokes.explanation: Touching and funny portrayal of Nelson's deprived childhood. The specific catalog detail and 'people in sweaters' shows his outsider perspective on normal family life.

jokes.text: So, my mom works at Hooters, but her dream is to go to Vegas and work in a real strip club.
jokes.setup: Nelson sharing about his family
jokes.punchline: The casual revelation of his mother's 'career aspirations'
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor about Nelson's dysfunctional family

jokes.text: He went out for a pack of cigarettes and never came back. He said 'smell ya later,' but he never smelled me again.
jokes.setup: Nelson explaining his absent father
jokes.punchline: Taking the casual farewell literally
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy that's both heartbreaking and hilarious. The literal interpretation of 'smell ya later' is perfectly innocent child logic applied to abandonment. One of the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: That's how we get our Kaypro. Sometimes I think he's more interested in his 'Itchy and Mitchy' cartoons than me.
jokes.setup: Nelson explaining how they got their computer/Marge complaining about Bart
jokes.punchline: Computer theft as casual backstory/Marge getting cartoon name wrong
jokes.explanation: Good character moments - Nelson's casual criminality and Marge's parental cluelessness about pop culture.

jokes.text: That's because your butt blocks your ears. That doesn't make any sense. Neither does your butt.
jokes.setup: Bart continuing butt insults
jokes.punchline: Doubling down with illogical retort when called out
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the running gag. Bart's non-logic when confronted is character-appropriate stubbornness.

jokes.text: Now, I know there's a handsome young man behind all that hair and dirt. Maybe not, but you did a great job on the lawn.
jokes.setup: Marge's optimistic assessment of cleaned-up Nelson
jokes.punchline: Immediate retreat to focusing on his work instead
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of expectation - Marge's polite optimism immediately deflated by reality, but she pivots gracefully to praising his work.

jokes.text: Here's the money you gave my son. We don't want your charity. I didn't give your son charity. I gave him self-respect.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Muntz returning money angrily
jokes.punchline: Marge's unexpectedly sharp comeback about giving self-respect instead of charity
jokes.explanation: Shows Marge can be assertive when defending her mothering, with a genuinely good comeback

jokes.text: Go home to your son, Mrs. Muntz, and try not to have intercourse on the way!
jokes.setup: Marge getting angry at Nelson's mother
jokes.punchline: Marge's version of a scathing insult
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge moment - her attempt at a devastating insult is hilariously polite and specific. The formality of 'try not to have intercourse' is brilliantly character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Cow milk?!
jokes.setup: Marge offering Nelson milk
jokes.punchline: Nelson's amazement at this luxury
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Nelson's poverty - regular milk is exotic to him. Simple but effective social commentary.

jokes.text: I'm your new roommate. I call top bunk. But... it's a single bed. I said top bunk!
jokes.setup: Nelson moving into Bart's room
jokes.punchline: His aggressive insistence despite the logical impossibility
jokes.explanation: Great Nelson bully logic - intimidation over physics

jokes.text: Be nice to Nelson, and I'll give you a Tootsie Pop.
jokes.setup: Marge's extended metaphor about tough exteriors and sweet interiors
jokes.punchline: Reducing it to simple bribery with candy
jokes.explanation: Great payoff to the metaphor - Marge's philosophical explanation reduced to basic parental bribery. Good structure and character work.

jokes.text: My teeth hurt all the time. I just try to think about motorcycles.
jokes.setup: Question about whether bottle opening hurts Nelson's teeth
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation of chronic pain and coping mechanism
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy - Nelson's matter-of-fact acceptance of constant pain with his touchingly innocent coping mechanism. Great character development.

jokes.text: A doctor for your teeth? What's next, a lawyer for your hair?
jokes.setup: Marge mentioning a dentist
jokes.punchline: Nelson's confusion about specialized professionals
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist logic showing Nelson's unfamiliarity with basic healthcare. The 'lawyer for your hair' escalation is perfectly illogical.

jokes.text: I gotta do something about him.
jokes.setup: Nelson's emotional singing performance
jokes.punchline: Bart's understated response to the dramatic moment
jokes.explanation: Simple reaction beat that works as contrast to Nelson's emotional moment. Functional but not particularly creative.

jokes.text: If I can have one dab of frosting and then stop, I'll know I've conquered food at last.
jokes.setup: Lisa testing her self-control with food
jokes.punchline: Setting up her inevitable failure
jokes.explanation: Good setup for eating disorder humor sequence

jokes.text: Well, at least she's not singing Streisand.
jokes.setup: Lisa's continued problematic eating
jokes.punchline: Homer's misplaced priorities about what's concerning
jokes.explanation: Good callback with Homer's typical misplaced concerns

jokes.text: I have a sister... I think she's dead now who had the same problem. And I know just the solution.
jokes.setup: Nelson offering to help Lisa with bullies
jokes.punchline: Callback to his dead sister comment
jokes.explanation: Good callback that reinforces the dark humor while moving plot forward. Works better on rewatch.

jokes.text: He who haw haws last, haw haws best.
jokes.setup: Nelson's triumphant moment
jokes.punchline: His modification of the classic saying with his signature laugh
jokes.explanation: Excellent Nelson character moment incorporating his signature laugh

jokes.text: So, now that you're happy again, I guess you won't beat me up at school anymore. I wish it were that simple. I really do.
jokes.setup: Bart hoping Nelson's happiness will end the bullying
jokes.punchline: Nelson's genuine regret that his nature won't change
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment that's both funny and poignant. Nelson's self-awareness and genuine regret about his bullying nature adds depth while maintaining humor.

jokes.text: Like many women, I still have an unhealthy obsession with my weight.
jokes.setup: Lisa reflecting on what she learned
jokes.punchline: Her clinical but unresolved self-analysis
jokes.explanation: Good meta moment about the episode's lack of resolution

jokes.text: Come on, say something conclusive. I'm afraid this is a very open-ended problem. Oh, open-ended?!
jokes.setup: Marge wanting a neat resolution to Lisa's body image issues
jokes.punchline: Lisa's realistic assessment frustrated by TV ending expectations
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-comedy about TV show expectations vs. real problems. The Simpsons commenting on its own format conventions while addressing serious issues.

tmdb_overview: Marge finds Nelson and acts as a mother figure towards him due to her children's loss of interest in her. Meanwhile, Lisa gets teased about her big butt, and becomes obsessed with her weight.
